encounter_context.encounter_reason: 9-year-old girl with moderate-persistent asthma and peanut anaphylaxis history presenting with acute wheeze after exposure to a known peanut trigger.

active_problems: Acute asthma exacerbation with oxygen desaturation to 90% on room air
Recent peanut ingestion with one dose of epinephrine administered by caregiver
recent_changes: Caregiver gave one IM epinephrine auto-injector 20 minutes before arrival.
Maintenance inhaled corticosteroid was skipped for the last 3 days.

medications.name: Albuterol nebulizer
medications.why_important: Primary reliever during exacerbation; intervals must be maintained until wheeze improves.

medications.name: Oral prednisolone
medications.why_important: Systemic corticosteroid course must continue at home to prevent late-phase relapse.

medications.name: Epinephrine auto-injector
medications.why_important: Peanut anaphylaxis with pre-hospital epinephrine requires monitoring for biphasic reaction.

safety_critical_facts: Peanut allergy with anaphylaxis — avoid any peanut exposure during and after admission.
One pre-hospital epinephrine dose given — monitor for biphasic anaphylaxis for at least 4 hours.
Complete the 5-day oral prednisolone course to prevent late-phase relapse.
disposition_and_follow_up: Observe in ED for 4 hours post-epinephrine, then discharge with asthma action plan if stable.

supporting_source_excerpts.label: Triage note
supporting_source_excerpts.text: Wheezing, SpO2 90% on room air, caregiver reports one epinephrine auto-injector given 20 min ago.

supporting_source_excerpts.label: Allergy history
supporting_source_excerpts.text: Peanut anaphylaxis documented, previous ED visit last year with biphasic reaction.
